bottom_line: An Avalanche Warning is in effect in all zones of our advisory area. Very dangerous avalanche conditions exist today and will likely continue during a period of prolonged warming. Avoid traveling in avalanche terrain today.
discussion:
We are continuing an Avalanche Warning for the Swan Range, the Whitefish Range, the Flathead Range, and portions of Glacier National Park through 0600 tomorrow (Monday). If conditions dictate, we may extend this warning to Tuesday. As of Sunday mid-morning, temperatures at many mid- and upper-elevation stations had been above freezing for 48 hours, reaching 50F at two upper-elevation stations yesterday.
The abnormally warm temperatures triggered deep hard slab avalanches on shaded slopes and thinner slabs on sunny aspects. Friday, the BNSF Avalanche Safety Team reported 3 large slab avalanches observed from Highway 2. Cam observed a D2.5 hard slab avalanche on a northwest aspect of Great Northern, and outside our forecast region, Rob viewed several large slab avalanches on sunny aspects of the Swan Crest near Condon. Saturday, blase observed multiple slab avalanches in the eastern portion of the advisory area, while Rob and I noted slides in the mountains around Lake McDonald. WMR patrol closed all their avalanche terrain as the day warmed. 
Our multi-day warming raises the risk of wet slab avalanches. Melting surface snow produces water that drains into the snowpack and may eventually reach the ground unless an impediment like a thick crust or weak layer is encountered. Here, the meltwater pools and increases the chance of releasing a wet slab avalanche. The meltwater production occurs faster on sunny slopes, where there are more crusts to interrupt the draining process. The general rule of thumb regarding this process is that three nights without a freeze are needed for meltwater production to drain into the snowpack. If forecasted temperatures materialize, we may begin seeing wet slab activity in the next day or two, although timing these is difficult. 
Planning an early exit and choosing low-angle terrain free of overhead hazards are ways to manage your risk in the face of uncertainty. If you observe avalanche activity, submit an observation to let us know what you are seeing.

bottom_line: An Avalanche Warning is in effect in all zones of our advisory area. Very dangerous avalanche conditions exist today, and will likely continue during a period of prolonged warming. Avoid traveling in avalanche terrain today.
discussion:
Ready or not, the heat is on for this weekend. We have issued an Avalanche Warning for the Swan Range, the Whitefish Range, the Flathead Range, and parts of Glacier National Park from 6 am today through 6 am tomorrow (Sunday). Remote weather stations have recorded above-freezing temperatures since yesterday, creating a lack of refreezing overnight. Heat and radiation destabilize thick, hard slabs, weaken cornices, and create widespread areas of wet surface snow.
We are most concerned about hard slab avalanches failing on buried weak layers. This Persistent Slab problem is unpredictable, and the consequences of getting caught in this type of slide are deadly. Observations from yesterday (Friday) included the BNSF Avalanche Safety Team’s report of 3 large slab avalanches observed from Highway 2. FAC Forecaster Cam observed a D2.5 hard slab avalanche on a northwest aspect of Great Northern. Outside of our forecast region, Rob, FAC Forecaster, viewed several large slab avalanches on the west, southwest, and south aspects of the Swan Crest.  These slides likely failed in the late afternoon yesterday. 
Natural and triggered avalanches involving wet surface snow are very likely on both sunny and shady slopes. We expect widespread wet loose activity today. Additionally, this heat wave will weaken ridgeline cornices and increase the likelihood of them unexpectedly breaking and subsequently triggering large to very large avalanches on the slopes below.  Planning an early exit and choosing low-angle terrain are ways to manage your risk in the face of uncertainty. If you observe avalanche activity, let us know what you are seeing by submitting an observation.

bottom_line: Danger will rise today due to rapid warming and intense sunshine. Large avalanches may occur naturally and run long distances. Move off of and out from under steep slopes during the heat of the day.
discussion:
Yesterday's wet loose action was more fizzle than sizzle. Today should bring on the heat in earnest, though crusts from previous warming could still limit the extent of today’s activity. Our last prolonged warming occurred late January into early February and resulted in a widespread avalanche cycle on a buried weak layer. Very large hard slabs failed early during the warming, followed by a cycle of wet slabs. That same event yielded the stout 2/3 crust.
Since then, a layer of facets near the 2/3 crust has produced sporadic action in our advisory area and beyond. It has also caused fatalities North, West, and South of our region. As we enter a period of prolonged warming, the stage is set for increased activity on buried weak layers, in addition to typical springtime problems like wet loose and cornice fall.
Avalanche Canada is painted red today, and all zones are expected to reach HIGH avalanche danger this weekend. Large human-triggered avalanches occurred near Fernie this week. Areas North have received more snow recently, and buried weak layers have been generally more reactive from the onset. Still, the slew of accidents and fatalities across the northwest US and Canada are a good reason to default to conservative terrain choices. Today, make it less about riding big terrain and more about laying in that base tan.

author: Guy Zoellner

bottom_line: Warm temperatures and direct sun will create dangerous avalanche conditions today. Rollerballs and snow falling out of trees are your clue that a wet loose avalanche cycle is starting. You can still trigger large avalanches on shaded slopes. Ridgelines and gentle terrain will provide the safest conditions and best opportunities for stunning views today.
discussion:
It’s all about trade-offs. Just when the forecast area seemed to be filled in and skiing/riding great, the clouds part and give way to warming temperatures and changing problems. 
The tricky part about the next few days is going to be managing wet snow and the lingering danger of persistent weak layers. As the snow on the sunny slopes transitions to wet, you will be forced onto more polar aspects. This shaded terrain still harbors several faceted crusts. Hard slabs failing on persistent weak layers have been well documented in the Whitefish Range and the Flathead Range over the last few weeks. We haven’t received any reports of natural or triggered avalanches in the Swan Range on these layers. Tuesday, an avalanche that failed on a buried weak layer, likely formed in early February, claimed the life of a backcountry skier near Lost Trail Pass south of Missoula.
With the sunset of the winter season just around the corner, we will have to hunt out the last powder stashes. As these get more affected by the warm weather, we’ll be driven to the precise slopes that harbor the recipe for large avalanches. Keep this in mind as FOMO begins to influence our decision-making.
As we transition into springtime travel, recall the cast of spring problems. Cornices that have grown over the last several weeks will begin to weaken due to sun and warm temperatures.  Limit your time underneath these hazards and stay well back from the edge while on ridgelines.

author: Jeff Metsky

bottom_line: Recent snow and strong winds thickened slabs above weak layers that caused very large natural avalanches last week. You may not get feedback like cracking or collapsing before triggering a destructive avalanche. Today, sticking to slopes 30 degrees or less will help to minimize your risk.
discussion:
Yesterday, (Tuesday) freezing levels reached roughly 6000 feet. Cooler temps overnight should keep the wet snow problem off the list for one more day. Recent wind speeds were strong enough to transport new and recent snow across the forecast area.  
You may not see signs of instability or recent avalanches but still trigger a potentially destructive avalanche. Conditions become more complex as you climb in elevation. If you choose to travel on NW-N-E-SE aspects, consider the overlapping problems at mid and upper elevations. These are the places where conservative terrain choices: (ex. <35 degrees, deep snowpack, planar slopes) will help you make it home to ride another day.
Digging a snowpit or probing the snow to identify weak layers below you are helpful, but realize this is only one data point on a slope, and your results can be different only a few feet away. How these buried layers will react to the weight of a rider or machine is difficult to predict. Yesterday, (Tuesday) Guy and I visited the site of a human-triggered avalanche in the Southern Whitefish Range (obs). The rider triggered a large avalanche (D2.5) on the February 3 crust. Judging by a set of tracks near the crown, it appeared that the rider hit a thin part of the slab, which likely triggered the avalanche. Fortunately, no one was caught or carried in this avalanche.  Identifying thin spots on a slope is nearly impossible at times. 
As we move into a period of drier and warmer temperatures over the next couple of days, the potential for wet snow avalanches will increase at all elevations. Shallow wet loose avalanches may run quickly and farther than you expect on top of recently formed crusts. Consider adjusting travel times as activity will increase in the afternoon hours.

bottom_line: Give avalanche-prone terrain a wide margin for error today, especially as you climb in elevation. Avalanches in the new snow may trigger a thicker, more dangerous avalanche. Safe options for riding are on slopes 30 degrees or less. Avoid dawdling in the runouts of large avalanche paths.
discussion:
Storm totals are starting to tally up at remote weather stations. In the past 48 hours, Noisy Basin in the Swan Range picked up .7 inches of Snow Water Equivalent (SWE), Stahl in the northern Whitefish Range picked up 1.2 inches of SWE, and Flattop in central Glacier National Park picked up 1.4 inches SWE. Tunnel Ridge in the Flathead Range gained 10 inches of settled snow since the start of the storm. The driver is the southwest winds that accompanied this precipitation. Winds turned out gusts 40 to 50 mph at remote weather stations. A few more inches and increased winds will thicken recently formed slabs.
There are a couple of different faceted crusts to keep an eye on. The first one you will see in the snowpack is 1 to 2 feet below the surface. Reported avalanche activity on this layer is more common in the Whitefish Range. The second layer is the one responsible for producing the largest avalanches. It is faceted snow above the early February crust buried 2 to 4+ feet deep. The Flathead Range and Glacier National Park are the hotspots for reported avalanche activity failing at this interface.
Weak snow above the early February crust has a bad reputation throughout the northwest. It extends north into Canada, south to Missoula, and west to Washington. Unfortunately, this interface is the culprit of close calls, accidents, and fatalities. This history continues to be the topic of conversation within the forecast team.

bottom_line: Triggering a large avalanche remains a possibility—primarily in wind-affected terrain. In these areas, a 2-4 foot dense slab may exist atop buried weak layers. To reduce your risk, avoid slopes with evidence of wind such as fresh drifts or a stiffer snow surface that supports your sled or skis.
discussion:
Overnight, a warm and windy storm brought snow to favored locations. Precipitation at SNOTEL sites varied from 0.5 inches at Noisy Basin and Stryker Ridge to 0.7 inches at Stahl Peak and 0.8” at Flattop in central Glacier Park. Tunnel Ridge leads in new snow depth with 8 inches of settled snow. Light snow continues today; however, the primary driving force of the avalanche hazard will be the moderate to strong southwest winds.
Fresh wind slabs will continue building throughout the day as elevated southwest winds buffet our area. Recorded wind gusts in the past 24 hours include 60 mph at Hornet, 94 mph at Tunnel, and 53 mph at Aeneas Ridge.
Among the few observations we received on Sunday, skiers and riders mainly mentioned the distinct snow/rain line at 4000 feet. Low elevations in our forecast area saw decreases in the snowpack as the warm storm continued overnight.
The Flathead Range continues to have a majority of large-to-very-large avalanche activity. Saturday’s good visibility brought more observations of these impressive crowns (photo, photo). However, since March 4th, we have had reports of 4 human-triggered slides in the Whitefish Range. Additionally, we have received word of an observation of large hard slab activity in Hay Creek in the Whitefish Range. If you're out and about and notice any large crowns, let us know so we can continue documenting the distribution of this problem.

As riders noted yesterday, plenty of dry snow from last week's storms remains available for transport, with more on the way later today. As you gain elevation, you will find thicker and larger slabs downwind of ridgelines and saddles and on the sidewalls of gullies. Today's blowing snow and cornice formation point toward slopes with developing slabs. Look for clues that the wind has been at work, such as drifts, dunes, rippled snow surfaces, and snow pillows. Cracks shooting away from your boards or machine indicate you have found an unstable slab directing you to sheltered or lower-angle terrain.\xa0"}]
bottom_line: Triggering a 2-4 foot thick avalanche on a buried weak layer remains possible in the Flathead Range and Glacier Park. You will most likely affect this layer on shaded slopes harboring a shallow snowpack. Blustery conditions will continue forming thin slabs of wind-drifted snow that could be reactive to a rider's weight.
discussion:
Yesterday (Saturday), temperatures reached 40F+ at many mid-elevation weather stations and 50F+ at low-elevations. Despite the mild conditions, high clouds and breezy conditions mostly kept the wet loose problem in check. One party did report triggering a small wet slide near the bottom of the Skook Chutes, while another noted rollerballs (photo). With cooler temperatures on tap for today, we have removed this problem from the list. However, you could probably trigger a small wet loose at low elevations where the temperature has remained above freezing since late morning yesterday. 
Winds increased yesterday afternoon, and last week's snow began transporting (photo). Winds cranked up overnight with a gust of 92 at Tunnel Ridge and into the 50s in several other locations. An approaching cold front will elevate the wind speeds and bring snow to help fuel the wind slab problem.
Sarah and Rob visited the site of two skier-triggered slides in the Chicken Bones area east of WMR from March 4 and 6. These occurred in an area cross-loaded by last week's strong winds and appeared to fail on a faceted weak layer below a melt-freeze crust 21 cm from the surface. 
In the Whitefish Range, several slides failed on buried weak layers last week (photo) and are clustered in a small area on the southeast side of this zone at mid-elevations. The crowns seem thinner than slides in the Flathead Range, so Jeff and Sarah will investigate one of these today and try to solve this puzzle.
The Flathead Range, interior Glacier Park, and southern Glacier Park are the epicenters of large-to-very-large avalanche activity. Good visibility allowed for more observations of crowns in this area yesterday (photo, photo).
A huge thank you to the 10 parties that submitted observations yesterday (Saturday). Riders found dry snow on shaded aspects and decent riding conditions on sunny aspects at mid and upper elevations, with generally stable snowpack tests noted by The Patrol Fund Level 1 class (observation, observation).

author: Mark Dundas

This problem is magnified in the mountains between Essex and Marias Pass, where the snowpack is thinner and weaker. Today's warming temperatures may make the slab above the weak snow more reactive to triggering.\xa0 You're most likely to trigger one of these beasts in steep, rocky, wind-swept terrain. Signs of instability, such as cracking or collapsing, may not be present before the slope you are on avalanches. Choosing lower-angle terrain without convexities is the easiest way to manage this problem.\xa0"}, {'problem_type': ' Wet Loose', 'problem_number': 2, 'likelihood': 'likely', 'size': ['Small (D1)', '', 'Large (D2)'], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': ['southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west'], 'middle': ['southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west'], 'lower': ['southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west']}, 'discussion': "Today's intense March sun and warming temperatures will increase the threat of wet loose avalanches, especially on steep, rocky, wind-sheltered terrain. These slides could entrain substantial snow and travel long distances on the underlying crust. Rollerballs, pinwheels, and snow falling from trees indicate warming and are precursors to loose snow slides.\xa0 \xa0\xa0 Though these slides will generally be small, tangling with one in long-running gullies, chutes, or large faces may make escape difficult. If you travel on or below steep sunny slopes, stay ahead of the sun and plan your exit strategy to avoid these areas in the afternoon. The shady side of the compass still holds dry, loose snow, which will keep you away from this problem and offer a higher-quality product.\xa0 \xa0"}]
bottom_line: Riders can trigger a large, destructive avalanche on a buried weak layer, especially on slopes with a thin snowpack or exposed rocks. Today's warming and sunshine may initiate loose snow slides as the surface softens. Rollerballs and mushy surface snow indicate you've worn out your welcome and need to move to a shadier aspect or stick to lower-angle terrain free of overhead hazards.
discussion:
Yesterday (Friday), cool morning temperatures, occasional high clouds, and light winds mostly kept the wet loose problem at bay. This morning, temperatures are 10-15 degrees warmer than yesterday, which may expedite the warming of the surface snow. The unknown is the amount of cloud cover that will make or break a wet loose cycle today. Generally, 6-12 inches of surface snow rests on a slick rain crust below around 5800 feet, with similar amounts above resting on old, harder snow. Yesterday's warming and solar input likely left a thin surface crust at low and mid-elevation sunny aspects. Wet loose activity will be delayed until the crust breaks down. An approaching cold front this afternoon will bring cloud cover and minimize this problem. 
Clear skies continue to allow observers unimpeded mountain views following recent snowfall and blustery winds. In the Swan Range, there has not been a documented avalanche on the early February buried weak layer, and we have trimmed the distribution of the Persistent Slab problem by removing the middle elevation band and dropped the likelihood of triggering at the upper elevations to unlikely. In the Whitefish Range, slides failing on buried weak layers are clustered in a small area on the southeast side of this zone at mid-elevations. These are located on the south and southeast aspects, and we have not had a chance to verify what layer they are failing on, as the crowns seem thinner than slides in the Flathead Range. The epicenter of large to very large avalanche activity is the Flathead Range, interior Glacier Park, and southern Glacier Park. 
Observations yesterday (Friday) noted no instabilities but found quality snow in sheltered locations in the Swan Range. An observer on Snowshed Mountain remotely triggered a cornice while kicking a separate section of cornice, but otherwise found no instabilities. A party in Rescue Creek found two faceted layers in the upper half of the pack, but they had no propagation in large column tests. Blase and I took a short walk up Rescue after our team meeting and enjoyed viewing impressive crowns and piles of debris from the February 28 and 29 loading events (photo, photo, photo). A party in central Glacier Park noted a whopper of a slide that failed on the early February crust (photo, photo).

Slabs can break 2 to 4 feet deep on facets formed in early February. The resulting avalanche will be difficult to escape. The most conservative option is to stick to lower-angled slopes in areas where the problem exists. Avoid convex rollovers and rocky, wind-effected slopes if you enter steeper terrain. Today's warming temperatures and solar radiation may stress weak layers buried in the snowpack.\xa0 Give steep terrain a wider margin during the heat of the day."}, {'problem_type': ' Wet Loose', 'problem_number': 2, 'likelihood': 'possible', 'size': ['Small (D1)', '', 'Large (D2)'], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': ['south', 'southwest', 'west'], 'middle': ['south', 'southwest', 'west'], 'lower': ['south', 'southwest', 'west']}, 'discussion': "A Wet Loose problem may develop today due to intense solar radiation and warming temperatures. Middle elevations may see the most activity, but upper elevations can't be ruled out. Move to lower-angle terrain or shaded aspects, IF you see signs of warming, like rollerballs and pinwheels scuttling down the hill.\xa0"}]
bottom_line: A snowmobiler triggered a large avalanche yesterday (Thursday) on a weak layer buried 2-4 feet deep. Warming temperatures can increase the potential for natural and human-triggered avalanches. Give steep slopes a wide margin, particularly during the heat of the day.
discussion:
This week I traveled to the Northern and Southern ends of the advisory area. In both outings, my party found seemingly benign conditions; limited effects from wind and sun, and unremarkable findings in our test pits. After wrapping up in one pit my partner commented, “If it wasn’t for all the action, we’d be riding everything.”
The action they were referring to consists of:

A handful of near-misses and accidents in the Marion Lake area during the President’s Day weekend
A skier-triggered hard slab on Slippery Bill Mountain in the Flathead Range on 2/26
A cycle of very large avalanches in the Flathead Range during the wind-loading event 2/28-2/29
Fatalities north and west of our advisory area

You can add Thursday's snowmobile-triggered avalanche in the Whitefish Range to this list. These slides all involved buried weak layers from early February. In some cases, the weak structure has been subtle and has fooled several parties including professionals. Stability tests have been a pretty mixed bag, although BNSF Avalanche Safety was able to reproduce propagating results in John F Stevens Canyon.  In this situation I'm placing more emphasis on behavior than appearance.  These buried weak layers may not look like much, but the above rap sheet has me wanting to steer clear.   
We removed the windslab problem as observations indicate tapering wind-loading and slabs trending toward unreactive. Continue to exercise caution where you find signs of wind-loading, like a scalloped surface or dune-shaped features. Recognize that previous wind-loading occurred on slopes harboring buried weak layers. Three human-triggered avalanches have occurred in the southern Whitefish Range since Monday. One of these (the previously mentioned snowmobile-triggered slide) stepped down to a buried weak layer and resulted in a larger avalanche.

bottom_line: Another human-triggered slab avalanche occurred in the southern Whitefish Range. Loose snow avalanches may become possible to trigger during the heat of the day on sunny aspects. Triggering an avalanche 2 to 4 feet thick is a low probability, high consequence scenario. Keep it simple by selecting lower-angled slopes.
discussion:
The southern Whitefish Range has been the focal point for recent human-triggered slab avalanches. Avalanches are failing up to a foot thick on slopes that face east and southeast. FAC will visit these sites soon to gather more snowpack information. 
The effects of solar radiation are growing as we work our way towards spring. Freezing levels are forecasted to climb to roughly 4500 feet. This will likely increase the chances of triggering loose snow avalanches. Thankfully, loose snow avalanche problems caused by solar radiation are typically easy to manage. Signs like rollerballs, snow falling out of trees, and gloppy snow are indicators of wet snow. 
Warming temperatures and solar radiation can increase the likelihood of triggering a slab avalanche. These environmental factors lead to rapid settlement and increased creep rates in the snowpack. This stresses weak layers and can cause them to fail. We have a few different facet/crusts on solar aspects, and a facet/crust responsible for several near-misses and accidents. The safest bet is to give steep terrain a wide margin for error.

bottom_line:
The primary concern is avalanches failing 2 to 4 feet deep on a persistent weak layer. Winds drifted snow into thicker, denser slabs above 5000 feet. Manage both problems by selecting lower-angled slopes on aspects that face NW-N-NE-SE.
Expect a Wet Loose avalanche problem IF the sun stays out longer than anticipated. Rollerballs and gloppy snow are signs of an impending Wet Loose avalanche cycle. Move to shaded slopes if you see this beginning.
discussion:
Avalanche activity in the last week has ranged from small to large (D1-D2), with a few very large (D3) avalanches. Most reported avalanches were Storm/Wind Slabs on slopes above 5000 feet. The larger avalanches are Persistent Slab avalanches failing 2 to 4 feet deep on a layer of facets above a hard crust buried in early February. Avalanche activity on this layer is most common above 6500 feet on aspects that face N-NE-SE.
Instabilities in the new and drifted snow should be relatively short-lived. Winds were transporting snow yesterday. Expect to see signs of cracking with the possibility of triggering smaller slabs on leeward-facing aspects. These new thin slabs rest over thick, hard slabs from the past week of moderate to strong winds. Although those are gaining strength, I’d still be wary of jumping onto steep slopes that face NW-NE-SE.
Persistent weak layers buried near the early February Crust remain untrustworthy. Snowpit tests continue to show mixed results of propagating and non-propagating. In some areas, this layer is buried at a depth where a human is less likely to trigger. On the flip side, if triggered, the resulting avalanche may be unsurvivable. Common trigger points are convex rollovers and thin parts of the slab. Unfortunately, it is sometimes impossible to identify where those thin parts are. Rocky outcroppings or areas scoured by the wind are the usual suspects.
The weather forecast suggests periods of clear skies. IF the sun stays out for longer than anticipated, or if you travel to the Whitefish Range which is supposed to see fewer clouds, then expect a loose snow avalanche problem on aspects getting hit by the warm sun. Sluffs, rollerballs, and gloppy snow are signs of a Wet Loose avalanche problem. Move to lower-angle slopes or slopes shaded by the sun if you see this problem beginning.

author: blase reardon
problems: [{'problem_type': ' Wind Slab', 'problem_number': 3, 'likelihood': 'possible', 'size': ['Small (D1)', '', 'Large (D2)'], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'northwest'], 'middle': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'northwest'], 'lower': []}, 'discussion': 'Today\'s third hazard lies in between the other two - apparent if you\'re looking, with the potential to bury or injure a person. The region has seen bouts of moderate to strong winds the past week that have built multiple generations of wind slabs. If winds pick up today as forecast, we may see another, as the low-density snow from Monday gets drifted. Patches of dense but soft snow near ridges will be the most sensitive to the weight of a person or snow machine. They may be covering older hard slabs of wind-packed snow that are harder for a person to trigger, but produce larger, more dangerous avalanches. These can step down and release very dangerous avalanches that break on the weak facets above the crust.\xa0 Look for dunes, drifts, and whale-shaped piles of hard snow below ridge crests and on the downwind sides of terrain features of rocks and gully walls. More subtle are drifts cross-loaded onto steep slopes, as shown by this unintentionally-triggered slide Monday east of Whitefish Mountain Resort. Hard, hollow-feeling snow isn\'t "bomber," it\'s a slab.\xa0'}, {'problem_type': ' Persistent Slab', 'problem_number': 1, 'likelihood': 'possible', 'size': ['Large (D2)', '', 'Very Large (D3)'], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'northwest'], 'middle': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'northwest'], 'lower': []}, 'discussion': "The most serious hazard today is the one to account for when choosing destinations, routes, and places to play. The lowest risk choices don't require travel under steep, shady start zones or riding up or down convex slopes where snow depths are likely to be variable. The potential for triggering avalanches from below lingers, and thin spots in the snow cover are potential trigger points for releasing hard slabs that would be very difficult to escape. A thin layer of weak, faceted snow is buried 2 to 4 feet deep, a few inches above a stout ice layer and/ or melt-freeze crust. While this layer is subtle in snowpits, the avalanches releasing on it are anything but. Multiple large to very large avalanches failed on this layer during the end of February storm and avalanche cycle. Many occurred on very steep, leeward slopes below ridges, where wind-transported snow built thicker, harder slabs over the facets. This upper elevation terrain remains the place where you're most likely to trigger an avalanche on the faceted layer.\xa0 At mid elevations, we've had almost no reports of avalanches, whumpfing collapses, or snowpit tests that show instability in the past week. However, fatal avalanche accidents to the west and north of our region likely involved this layer and occurred near 6500 feet, the boundary between our mid and upper elevations. That tells me that it's important to minimize exposure to this hazard in terrain approaching that elevation, to leave a margin for safety.\xa0 So pick destinations that offer options to ride sunny slopes where the weak layer is less common, especially because the riding on these slopes will deteriorate with the sun and warming temperatures headed our way. In shady terrain, stick to lower angled slopes that are planar or concave in profile. Park or stop well out of the runouts of steep start zones, even on what look like small slopes.\xa0 \xa0"}, {'problem_type': ' Dry Loose', 'problem_number': 2, 'likelihood': 'possible', 'size': ['Small (D1)', '', 'Large (D2)'], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': ['southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west'], 'middle': ['southeast', 'south', 'southwest', 'west'], 'lower': []}, 'discussion': "The most obvious hazard today will be the easiest to avoid. If the sun comes out this afternoon, expect sluffs of fresh snow to run on very steep slopes, triggered by snow falling off trees or rocks. Most will be harmless. The exceptions will be: If you're in or below this terrain and you see rollerballs fanning downslope, move on. If you want to ride steep, confined slopes, get down and out from under them before the sun hits."}]
bottom_line: Choose your destinations and play spots with an eye to avoiding today's trifecta of avalanche hazards. The most serious is triggering large, serious avalanches that break on weak snow buried 2 to 4 feet deep. Secondary hazards today include the potential for sluffing in the new snow, particularly if the sun comes out, and the possibility of triggering dense slabs of drifted snow. Careful attention to terrain will help you find a Goldilocks band of quality, low-risk riding.
discussion:
Problems, problems, problems. We've listed three avalanche hazards today. Recent obs describe a rain crust that extends to about 5000 feet and sometimes 5300 feet. Others, a sun crust on solar aspects, particularly southwest.
The Goldilocks band is above that rain crust, in shaded, wind-sheltered terrain that's not too steep and free of the lingering danger of triggering slides that break deep in the snowpack. Whew. It's half-caff semi-dry cappucino with oat milk. But being specific is the key to low risk, fun riding today. Check the problem descriptions for ideas how to maximize your your riding and safety today.
The general trend is decreasing danger, as weak layers slowly strengthen and adjust to the load delivered by this past weekend's storm. A ridge building in at the end of this week will interrupt that trend on sunny slopes. Clear skies and direct sun may produce a wet snow avalanche cycle on sunny slopes. Shaded slopes, however, will continue to stabilize so long as temperatures remain below freezing overnight. It's hard to say yet whether the ridge will bring a diurnal melt-freeze cycle strong enough to produce supportable crusts and corn on sunny slopes early in the day. Stay tuned.

bottom_line: Buried weak layers remain a concern. If triggered, these avalanches are likely very large and unsurvivable. Keep in mind that as you gain elevation, ridgetop terrain is affected by winds and may be reactive to the weight of your sled or boards.
discussion:
Reports of natural avalanches have continued to come in from the past week. Many of these avalanches are large to very large and have the characteristics of a persistent slab problem. Crowns and fracture lines are several feet thick; many of these slides are more than 100 feet wide. These slides likely failed during the intense wind-loading event on Wednesday night or Thursday morning. They likely failed on a layer of weak faceted snow above the early February crust. We expect to continue to catalog similar avalanches when we have clearing skies and visibility. 
Incremental snow accumulations and light winds are expected today. As many observers reported on Sunday, there have been few signs of instability during their tours. Two observers in the northern Whitefish Range had no propagating results in their snowpack tests but were alarmed to see a large avalanche with a crown of 3 feet (Red Meadow observation, Link observation). In the Flathead Range, multiple crowns of very large avalanches have been observed (photo 1, photo 2, photo 3), with all of these slides starting at upper elevations.
Observers completing a snow survey in Glacier National Park submitted photos of debris that impacted the Going-To-the-Sun-Road from the Red Rock Slide path (photo 1, photo 2). The debris appears to be wet loose from the Leap Day rain event. Red Rock is just upstream from Avalanche Creek and is a dramatic path that drops steeply for 3484 feet off the shoulder of Mt Cannon (photo). This path hits the road yearly and occasionally dams up the adjacent McDonald Creek.

bottom_line: Recently formed hard slabs may be masked by yesterday's snow. You're most likely to trigger a slide in terrain that the wind has loaded. You can reduce your risk of triggering slides today by avoiding steep, rocky, wind-affected slopes.
discussion:
Most locations experienced a brief, intense shot of precipitation Saturday. Widespread snow totals are generally 3-6 inches, with the northern Whitefish Range and the Flathead Range favored and the Swan Range a distant third.  Fortunately, temperatures have cooled since last week's valley floor 50F readings, with the snow line now below 1000 feet. Cold arctic air is spilling over the Continental Divide on the far eastern edge of our advisory area with -3F at Marias Pass this morning. 
Saturday, experienced backcountry groups found enjoyable riding in sheltered locations. A party in the northern Whitefish Range experienced up to 16 inches of dry, unconsolidated snow with no instabilities observed. A group in the southern Whitefish Range found the new storm snow touchy and noted multiple (10+) thin D1-size slabs in sheltered locations. They wisely maintained wide margins, only exposed themselves to nonconsequential terrain, and intentionally stuck to low-angle and low-consequence slopes throughout the tour. A group in the Flathead Range noted calm conditions in a drainage sheltered from the east winds. They had 3 inches of afternoon snowfall with some sluffing in the new snow, and 5300 feet seems to be the transition point from good skiing to more spicy.
FAC staff traveled to Cascadilla Creek to look closer at a very large avalanche on Nyack Mountain. Unfortunately, our visibility window was limited to a short period when we were still well down the valley from the slide. However, we did get enough of a view in the flat light to recognize this slide is very large, with a crown exceeding 1000 feet wide. This slide failed during the intense wind-loading event on Wednesday night or Thursday morning. It likely failed on a layer of weak faceted snow above the early February crust. We did note that rain had affected the low and middle elevations of this drainage, leaving behind a widespread surface crust (photo) and well-developed runnels (photo), which made skiing challenging.

bottom_line: Recent wind and snow have stressed weak layers buried deep in the snowpack. Decrease your risk by limiting travel on and below steep, wind-drifted slopes. Avalanches can break well above you, wider than expected, and will be difficult to escape.
discussion:
The snow surface took a beating this week from moderate to strong southwesterly winds. Yesterday (Friday), Blase and Sarah were East of WMR, where they noted improved weather and stability but generally worse riding conditions than the previous day’s. Although the winds have relented, and natural avalanche activity is unlikely today, it is still possible for a rider to trigger a deadly avalanche, particularly on upper-elevation leeward slopes.  
North of the advisory area, a Special Public Avalanche Warning is in effect for most of British Columbia’s and Alberta’s forecast regions. This warning concerns recent loading on “prominent weak layers established during drought conditions in February.” That should sound familiar since a similar structure has been found across our region.
We received less snow than our neighbors to the north, but we had plenty of wind to make up for it. Thursday, BNSF Avalanche Safety reported a handful of large to very large natural avalanches in John F Stevens Canyon. These slides likely ran sometime late on Wednesday, occurred on wind-loaded slopes, and several may have failed on buried weak layers. Friday, we received similar reports of several very large avalanches in the Middle Fork drainages. These recent slides indicate that the persistent weak layer has not fully healed and still demands caution.

bottom_line:
Conditions remain dangerous due to continued wind transport, recent snow, and weak layers buried deep in the snowpack. To minimize your risk, limit your travel on and below steep, wind-drifted slopes. Avalanches can break wider and run longer than expected.
The upper McDonald Valley received significantly more snow and the avalanche danger may be higher in central Glacier National Park.
discussion:
Yesterday (Thursday) was pretty wild: intense, buffeting wind and natural cornice fall on the ridge top, and 50*F with sticky snow down low. Somewhere in between, though, there’s good honest riding in wind-sheltered terrain.
Today should be one step closer to normal. Most stations are reporting seasonable temperatures, decreasing winds, and a trace of precipitation overnight. The one notable exception is in central Glacier National Park, where Flattop Mountain picked up another 1.3 inches of SWE in the last 24 hours. That totals 2.5 inches of water and 14+ inches of snow in 48 hours for that location. Slabs in the upper McDonald Valley will be thicker. 
Though the weather moderated some, the danger remains elevated. Winds are still sufficient to build slabs and add stress to buried weak layers. Yesterday (Thursday), BNSF Avalanche Safety reported a handful of large to very large hard slabs on wind-loaded slopes in John F Stevens Canyon. Several likely failed on the uncooperative facet layer near the early-February Crust. This layer continues to give mixed results in pit tests. It may not look like much, but for the time being, it still deserves some respect. When in doubt, opt for simple, low-angle terrain.

bottom_line: Heavy snow and strong winds have created very dangerous avalanche conditions. Continued snowfall and the possibility of rain-on-snow will keep the danger elevated through the day. Natural and triggered avalanches have the potential to break across terrain features and run longer than expected. Avoid traveling in avalanche terrain.
discussion:
Happy Leap Day! If you get to celebrate a real birthday for the first time in 4 years, enjoy. For the rest of us, we get an additional day to play in the snow. Unfortunately, as the snow totals stack up, the terrain for safe recreation becomes more limited.
Heavy snowfall combined with 3 days of southwest winds have left a variety of avalanche  problems for backcountry users to navigate. The answer to a complex weather pattern and multiple avalanche problems is terrain management. Seek out simple terrain that is under 30 degrees and not exposed to avalanches from above.
Temperatures have risen 10 to 15* F since snow began a few days ago. New and recent snow have developed into an upside-down slab. Think: stronger and warmer,  well-bonded snow over weak, cold, dry snow. Continued warming temperatures today (Thursday) will bring the rain/ snow line to 5000 feet and possibly 5500 feet. Rain on new snow rapidly destabilizes it, by adding heat and mass to slabs and rapid strain to buried weak layers, like our old frenemy from early February (the stout/crust facet combo). Finally, drifting snow on leeward aspects has formed reactive slabs along ridgelines and steep rollovers. If/when precipitation switches to rain, expect the avalanche danger to spike across all zones. If you find areas with 8 inches or more of recent snow, expect avalanches to be easily triggered by the weight of a rider or snowmachine.
The safest bet for today is to avoid traveling under avalanche paths or near connected terrain, as you could trigger avalanches from a distance or below.

bottom_line:
An Avalanche Watch is in effect until 18:00 this evening. Dangerous avalanche conditions will develop as winds and snowfall increase today and tonight. Fresh slabs will build on leeward terrain, and add stress to a weak layer buried 3-4 feet. Any avalanche today could entrain a large volume of snow, making escape difficult. Wind-sheltered, low-angle terrain will still provide enjoyable conditions. 
The danger may not rise to HIGH until sundown or tonight.
discussion:
Storm days are the best. The raw power and artistry of Mother Nature doing her thing are always fun to watch. The sculpting of the wind on recent snow will leave us with a new masterpiece in the mountains. Danger will rise throughout the day.  If the forecast verifies, we will reach HIGH sometime this evening.  We've listed upper elevations at HIGH in all zones in case things come in a little earlier or spicier than anticipated. 
There are several things to remember as we take in the fresh slate over the next few days.
1). There is uncertainty about the timing of the storm today and snowfall amounts.  Keep in mind that the hazard will INCREASE throughout the day.  IF storm totals for wind and snowfall deliver as predicted then the danger rating will rise to HIGH.
2). Don’t forget the buried weak layer. We haven’t received any reports lately of the facet/crust being reactive in stability tests. And we haven’t heard of an avalanche in our forecast area associated with this layer for a week. But people keep finding the structure present, and it is going to get a rapid load over the next 24 hours.  
3). There is plenty of snow available for transport and all three ranges are forecasted to see Southwest winds in the 30’s and an additional 10-20 inches of snowfall. Fresh wind-slabs will be easy to find over the next several days. Give these slabs a few days before venturing across them to find better snow in the sheltered terrain.
4). There is a chance of freezing levels reaching 5,000 feet.  If you start to see rollerballs in the lower elevations or snow falling out of the trees then seek out lower angle terrain.  
Storm days such as this are great times to get wheelies on the snowmobile locked in, or explore low angle old growth spruce powder turns. There is plenty of fun terrain to be had without entering up on slopes that are dealing with fresh loading.

author: blase reardon

Propagation in some snowpack tests before the storm indicates that these layers are not yet dormant. The additional load may have reactivated them. Minimize your time on and under steep slopes, particularly those that are convex in profile and that may be scoured by recent winds. Thin spots in the slabs are likely trigger points and are more common in slopes with these characteristics. You can trigger these slabs from below or from adjacent slopes, so keep an eye on your riding partners. Park well out of runouts so if a partner is surprised, you can keep eyes on them and minimize your search time.\xa0'}, {'problem_type': ' Wind Slab', 'problem_number': 1, 'likelihood': 'likely', 'size': ['', 'Large (D2)', ''], 'aspect_elevation': {'upper': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'south', 'northwest'], 'middle': ['north', 'northeast', 'east', 'southeast', 'south', 'northwest'], 'lower': []}, 'discussion': "Powerful winds have raked the Flathead Range, McDonald Valley, and Marias Pass the past two days, drifting snow into hard slabs that can react unpredictably to the weight of a rider or snowmachine. They can release well above a rider and break much wider than expected. The hard chunks of debris can deliver a serious pummeling to a person caught and carried. With continued loading today, natural avalanches remain possible in upper elevations. The debris from these slides can run into middle and low elevations. bottom_line: Strong winds have drifted recent snow into dense, unstable slabs that, because of variable wind directions, exist on a variety of aspects and elevations. Avoid the runouts of steep start zones well above you, particularly if you see blowing snow at upper elevations. You can still trigger large to very large slabs that break 2 to 3 feet deep, on thin layers of weak snow buried just above a stout, obvious crust. Be very suspicious of steep, shaded slopes at mid and upper elevations.
discussion:
New snow and powerful winds have left dense slabs of drifted snow across the forecast region the past few days. Storm totals (since Saturday morning) have varied, with the northern and eastern parts of the forecast region favored. I'm estimating 12 to 15 inches of storm snow in the northern Whitefish Range, 18 to 20 inches in the upper McDonald Valley in Glacier National Park, and a foot or more in the Flathead Range near Tunnel Ridge. The storm was stingy around Whitefish Mountain Resort and the in the Swan Range, where storm totals look to be 4 to 7 inches. 
The winds, however, were much more equitable. Most stations recorded hourly averages of 25 to 45 mph Sunday, with gusts of 40 to 80 mph - and over 100 mph at Tunnel Ridge. Wind speeds dropped to the single digits Monday when arctic air arrived. They look to veer back to the west and southwest today, and pick up again.
The storm's legacy today is dense slabs of drifted snow that are widespread in typically leeward terrain - NW to NE to SE - and isolated in usually windward terrain. The slabs are mostly composed of snow that fell during the storm, because sun crusts were present on windward slopes prior to the storm.  So the slabs will be thicker and larger in areas that got more snow, like the northern Whitefish Range, upper McDonald Valley, John F. Stevens Canyon, and the Flathead Range. You'll find them most common at upper elevations. Be on the lookout for dense slabs of drifted snow in unusual spots as well - well below ridges, on usually scoured sides of gullies, etc. The main danger with these hard slabs is that they break unpredictably, so you can get well onto a slab before it releases. 
These dense slabs of drifted snow are adding to the load above tricky weak layers now buried 1 to 3 feet below the snow surface and just a few inches above an obvious and very stout crust. These layers were the culprits in a series of accidents and near-misses in the Flathead Range over the holiday weekend. We've had no reports of triggerd slides on these layers in other ranges, though they are present and sometimes propagate in snowpack tests. These layers haven't given much warning prior to releasing  - no audible collapes and no cracking. I remain very leery of steep, shaded terrain in the Flathead Range. Elsewhere, snowpack tests might be more reliable. Sticking to slopes that are less than 35 degrees and aren't below steeper terrain is the most straightforward way of managing this hazard.

bottom_line: Strong winds will transport new snow building fresh slabs. Triggering a large avalanche is very likely—primarily in wind-affected terrain. To reduce your risk, avoid slopes with evidence of wind, such as fresh drifts or a stiffer snow surface that supports your skis or sled.
discussion:
We issued an Avalanche Warning for the Flathead Range/Glacier National Park zone on Sunday night, with the start of a powerful storm favoring this area. This zone will remain at high avalanche danger, as we expect natural avalanches to be likely and human-triggered avalanches to be very likely. Traveling in avalanche terrain today, specifically on leeward slopes at upper elevations, is not recommended. 
Strong winds are the driving force for avalanches in all areas of the forecast region. We’ve listed Wind Slabs as our primary problem. Ridgetop winds are cranking in the Flathead, with Tunnel Ridge recording 104 mph gusts at 4 am. Other regional stations include 78 mph gusts at Hornet and 66 mph at Aeneas Ridge and Snowslip. 
Warm temperatures associated with the start of this storm have brought impressive SWE totals. Flattop recorded 1.9 inches of water in less than 24 hours, while Stryker recorded 1 inch. The Swan Range missed out in this storm, only picking up 0.3 inches.
We expanded the distribution of the lurking Persistent Slab problem to include middle elevations where we’ve observed the facet/crust structure. Impressive wind drifting with the current storm is loading slopes harboring this layer.  This additional weight could be the tipping point for slabs to fail. 
Traveling today in the mountains will require monitoring winds and snow. If you are able to keep your hat on, let us know what you see by submitting an observation.

bottom_line:
Today's incoming storm will develop dangerous avalanche conditions in areas favored by snowfall and wind. Expect the danger to rise as snow totals increase throughout the day and overnight. Fresh slabs will form initially on wind-drifted slopes and in sheltered locations later in the day. Cracking beneath your boards or machine directs you to seek out lower-angle terrain.
The danger may not reach HIGH until sundown or overnight.
discussion:
A robust Pacific storm system will move into the area today (Sunday), with heavy snow and blustery winds creating dangerous avalanche conditions. Snowfall and windy conditions will continue overnight and into Monday, with a Winter Storm Warning in effect from noon Sunday until 0800 Monday. 
Snowfall yesterday and overnight favored the Flathead Range and John F. Stevens Canyon areas, with 5-7 inches. Most other locations were skunked.
Today's rising danger is related to the incoming snowfall. This morning, most Swan and Whitefish Range slopes will have a generally stable snowpack. The danger will increase after several inches of new snow accumulates. The danger in the Flathead Range and southern Glacier Park is slightly elevated due to recent snow, and the danger will rise sooner in this zone.
Today's storm will favor the Flathead Range, southern Glacier Park, and interior Glacier. The avalanche danger may reach HIGH later today or overnight in these locations. Forecasted totals for other areas are less. The northern Whitefish Range will receive more than the slopes near the WMR ski area.  The Swan Range may only receive totals similar to the southern Whitefish Range. We anticipate the danger reaching CONSIDERABLE in these locations. 
Monitor changing conditions and snow totals throughout the day. With the impressive wind speeds, higher quality riding will be found in terrain sheltered from the wind.

bottom_line:
Triggering a large avalanche remains a possibility—primarily in wind-affected terrain. In these areas, a slab may exist atop a buried weak layer. To reduce your risk, avoid slopes with evidence of wind, such as fresh drifts or a stiffer snow surface that supports your sled or skis.
A strong storm will impact the area beginning Sunday, creating dangerous avalanche conditions.
discussion:
A robust Pacific storm system will move into the area on Sunday, with heavy snow and blustery winds creating dangerous avalanche conditions. If the forecast verifies, we may reach CONSIDERABLE danger on Sunday and perhaps HIGH on Monday. Unsettled weather continues throughout the work week, keeping the hazard elevated and new snow avalanche problems on the list. 
Today (Saturday), we will primarily experience the effects of wind, with the chance of light snow showers developing this afternoon. Due to moderate sustained west and southwest winds, we added wind slab to the problem list. Overnight wind speeds were impressive, with gale force gusts (greater than 38 mph) at many upper elevation stations, with 88 mph recorded at Tunnel Ridge. Despite the limited snow available for transport, riders noted wind-drifted snow filling in their skin track along with small reactive cornices in southern Glacier Park.
The last recorded avalanche on a buried weak layer occurred on Monday, 5 days ago. This week's relatively mild temperatures are helping to strengthen this layer found 1 to 2 feet below the surface. Today's wind loading will initially test this weak snow, but the heart punch begins tomorrow with the heavy snow and drifting.  
In the southern Whitefish Range, three groups from the Patrol Fund Level 2 avalanche course submitted observations from just east and west of the ski area (Observation 1, Observation 2, Observation 3). Numerous snowpit tests resulted in non-propagating results except for an ECTP 28 down 37 cm on a south aspect. They looked but could not locate the buried surface hoar (photo) that Rob noted Thursday. 
Friends Fest is Here! Today, February 24th, is the last day—Hot Dog Days at the Canyon Creek trailhead and the top of Flower Point chair at WMR. Don't miss the Ultimate Après with Backcountry Partner Speed Dating and the Big A$$ Raffle. More info at flatheadavalanche.org/event/friendsfest

author: Rob Millspaugh

This layer is not distinct on some slopes, though the crust and ice are apparent. The hazard exists primarily at upper elevations on steep, shaded slopes, and resulted in two recent accidents and two near misses. You can find this hazard on isolated slopes elsewhere, including middle elevations and solar aspects. Reduce your risk through careful terrain selection during your climb, traverse, or descent. Evaluate slope angles, slope shapes, potential trigger points, and the consequences of a slide.\xa0 Good options include slopes that are less than 35 degrees in steepness, concave (bowl-shaped) or planar in shape, and not peppered with rocks and trees or punctuated by cliff bands, gullies, or abrupt benches.\xa0'}]
bottom_line: On upper-elevation, shaded slopes, you can trigger slabs that break one to two feet deep without obvious signs of instability. Decrease your risk by choosing terrain with a consistent slope angle and free from hazards like cliffs, gullies, and exposed rocks and trees. Travel one at a time and watch your partner from a safe location.
discussion:
Once bitten, twice shy: that’s my attitude toward the current persistent slab problem. With two accidents and two near misses in the last week, I feel a bit shier than I usually might at Moderate danger. 
Besides these incidents, the weak structure has not given obvious signs of instability, like cracking or collapsing. Furthermore, this structure has been challenging to identify in pit profiles and tests, although more recent observations are finding propagation in ECTs. The weak layer presents as a layer of rounding facets and patchy surface hoar 1-2 feet below the surface and a few inches above the stout and well-documented crust from early February. In some cases, it is barely softer than the overlying slab. It has fooled experienced parties, including FAC forecasters.
During my brief tour outside of WMR yesterday (Thursday), I had the luxury of finding an obvious layer of buried surface hoar roughly one foot deep, which propagated with moderate force. This prominent layer appears to be the exception and not the norm. Elsewhere, I'm placing less emphasis on my interpretation of the snowpack and more on spatial variability and uncertainty. The recent incidents and close calls are good reasons to reevaluate and consider increasing your margins by dialing back to less aggressive terrain. 
With minimal new snow, we've elected to drop the wind slab problem. Winds at Aeneas Ridge in the Swan Range, Hornet in the Whitefish Range, and along the continental divide have remained adequate for transport and may increase throughout the day. If you are in one of these areas and notice the hallmarks of wind-effected snow, like a scalloped surface or dune-shaped features, consider moving to less steep or more sheltered terrain. Warm temperatures and sunshine in today's forecast mean there is potential for some small wet loose activity; however, this again will be limited by scant snow on crusty solar aspects. Pay attention to the weather and the snow surface throughout the day. If you're on a southerly slope with more than 8 inches of loose snow transitioning to wet, it's probably time to find less steep or less sunny terrain.
Friends Fest is Here! February 21st-24th: Check out Workshop Night (Tonight) with State of the Snowpack and USGS, Hot Dog Days, Ultimate Après with Backcountry Partner Speed Dating, and the Big A$$ Raffle. More info at flatheadavalanche.org/event/friendsfest

Most of these slabs have formed over the past two days; the slabs could continue developing in areas that receive more than a few inches of snow today. Check the runout of the slope - free of things that can magnify the consequences of getting caught and carried down?\xa0 If the answer to either question is yes, step back and consider alternatives on wind-sheltered, low-angle slopes.'}]
bottom_line: The best riding with the lowest risk of triggering a slab avalanche exists on shaded, mid-elevation slopes. In this terrain, stick to slopes less than 35 degrees that have runouts free of terrain traps. Elsewhere, a sun crust may make for less quality riding. And on upper-elevation, shaded slopes, you can trigger slabs that break one to two feet deep with little prior signs of instability.
discussion:
An innocuous-looking layer of facets and surface hoar continues to pose a danger across the region. It's been the weak layer in at least two accidents - one mine - and two near-misses in the past week. 
While this structure exists across the region, we can narrow down the terrain where you're most likely to get into trouble, based on accident sites and recent observations. The most likely suspects:

Face north to northeast at upper elevations
Are in the Flathead Range
Are more than 35 degrees in steepness and aren't adjacent to steeper slopes
Are convex in profile

Again, that's the most likely terrain. One unintentionally-triggered slide occurred on a north-northwest facing slope, and another was triggered from a spot that was 33 or 34 degrees. People have reported the structure on mid-elevation slopes in all ranges, where it's propagated in snowpack tests. During the storms that loaded the weak layer, remotely-triggered slides broke on this layer in the Swan Range. A few naturals have also released on southeast-facing slopes. Several of the unintentionally-triggered slides also appear to have released where the slab was thinner, possibly because winds scoured away some of the slab.
I'm focusing on these characteristics because terrain-based decisions are the most reliable way of avoiding surprises and trouble. The slab above the facets and surface hoar is relatively soft and the weak layer is spatially variable. In those conditions, snowpack tests tend to be less accurate predictors of instability. And none of the parties involved in the past week's incidents reported collapsing or cracking prior to the slides. 
A secondary, short-lived hazard today is triggering small wind slabs. These aren't widespread, and most are shallow and small. And they're definitely more predictable. Nonetheless, they can be dangerous in steep, consequential terrain, like couloirs and chutes and steep faces. Check for this hazard with thorough assessments before committing to steep, consequential terrain.

bottom_line: Avalanches may still fail at a weak layer buried a few inches above a very hard crust. Overnight winds and a few inches of snow have formed thin pockets of wind drifted snow below ridgelines. Low angle slopes that are protected from the wind will be the most enjoyable today.
discussion:
Patience is the name of the game with today’s problems.  A Persistent Slab avalanche  structure exists across the forecast region. The primary concern is a layer of weak, faceted snow buried 12 to 24 inches below the surface and a few inches above the early February crust/ ice layer. The layer appears most developed in the Flathead Range, where slabs have been  reactive to the weight of skiers multiple times over the last 4 days (here and here). Several of these slides have been triggered from spots where the slab is thinner. The same facet and crust combo has been reported in the Whitefish and Swan Ranges. However, we’re not getting any reports of triggered avalanches in these 2 ranges. In the Flathead Range incidents, the parties observed no collapsing or cracking prior to triggering the slides. The take home message is that this weak layer may provide very little feedback, until it is breaking underneath you or your machine.  
Temperatures hovering around freezing and cloudy skies this morning mean conditions ought to be ripe for traveling on your favorite ridgeline today or riding from one drainage to the next.  If the sun does break through, or if the temperature exceeds the forecast, beware of loose snow avalanches.  Clues will be rollerballs and sticky skins. The Flathead Range has seen most south aspects get cooked lately. The Northern Whitefish and Swan ranges still harbor soft snow that hasn't been affected by the sun. This slight warming could also speed settlement and consolidation of the slab above the facets. Find small test slopes and terrain free of hazards below before commiting to a line if you feel this happening.  We have yet to see the facet/crust combo in the Whitefish and Swan Ranges tested like in the Flathead Range.

bottom_line: Large human-triggered avalanches occurred within the last 24 hours. Stick to lower angled slopes on shaded aspects above 6500 feet. New snow and southwest winds will form thin slabs of drifted snow. Windward aspects are safer options for riding.
discussion:
Today, southwest winds will drift the couple of inches of new snow that falls. Expect thin and small slabs to form on leeward-facing aspects. A sun crust will limit the amount of snow available for transport, so drifting should be fairly minimal. The aspects being drifted are the same aspects where people have triggered large avalanches failing on a persistent weak layer 1 to 2 feet below the surface.
Reported human-triggered avalanches are confined to shaded aspects above 6500 feet in the Flathead Range. I suggest dialing slope angles back wherever you feel a hard crust buried more than a foot below the surface. Signs of instability and snowpack tests are not providing negative feedback, which may dupe a person into thinking the slope is stable. Here is an example.
Yesterday, Blase and I visited the crown of a recent human-triggered avalanche. This avalanche failed on facets a few inches above the early February crust. During our descent, we unintentionally triggered a neighboring slope. The start of this slope had a planar/concave shape with slope angles in the low 30s. The slope was relatively short and sparsely vegetated. Prior to the slide, we observed no signs of unstable snow on neighboring slopes and no alarming results in tests. The one red flag was the nearby avalanche.  The first rider on the slope triggered the slide just below the ridgeline and could not escape the moving debris. The rider was caught, carried, and lost some equipment. 
Experienced teams and veteran forecasters are getting surprised. This is not a problem to try to outsmart. Keep it simple.

bottom_line: A lurking weak layer in the snowpack has resulted in recent large avalanches. Traveling in the mountains today requires the knowledge that there is still a possibility of triggering a 1-2 foot thick slab avalanche. Choose terrain free of convexities to limit your chance of triggering a slide.
discussion:
On Saturday, three separate skier-triggered avalanches in the Marion Lake area were symptoms of a Persistent Slab problem.  These slides resulted in one incident which required a helicopter evacuation, as well as two close calls. As we investigated the characteristics of these avalanches, some themes emerged.
Temperature: Friday’s solar input began the initial warming of cold surface snow, which accelerated with Saturday's warmer temperatures. This transition from a loose surface to a consolidated slab was the missing ingredient for the Persistent Slab problem. An impressive temperature change is a hallmark of increasing instability within the snow. Regarding the three avalanches occurring on February 17th, all three slides occurred in the afternoon, around or slightly after the day’s peak warming.
Terrain: Skiers triggered slides on steep, northerly upper-elevation slopes with convex rollovers in all three avalanches.  These slopes have been the target of FAC’s messaging about the current Persistent Slab problem. The snowpack’s structure of weak faceted snow above a stout crust has been unpredictable, and multiple people have skied similar or adjacent slopes before triggering an avalanche. The lack of obvious signs of instability increases the need to pay attention to terrain features. Thin parts of the snowpack and convexities increase the probability of propagating an avalanche.
Community: All three parties involved in Saturday’s events were transparent and forthcoming with details of each incident. For the skier caught and carried, it was extremely fortunate to have ski partners who acted quickly and thoughtfully. Two Bear Air and ALERT Air Ambulance were invaluable resources that evacuated and transported the victim to the hospital for overnight care. 
Practicing safe travel habits can stack the odds in your favor. These habits include communicating and being aware of your partner’s intended travel.  Get in the habit of identifying potential avalanche paths and positioning yourself in a safe spot within the terrain. Unpredictable avalanches, such as the current Persistent Slab problem, should garner your respect. If you head out into the mountains today, be mindful of changing conditions, the slope aspects you ride, and where you and your partners are positioned in the environment.

bottom_line: Skier-triggered slides on Saturday confirm that releasing a 1-2 foot thick slab avalanche on a buried weak layer remains possible. Pay attention to changing snow surface conditions throughout your tour. Treat upper-elevation shaded slopes with respect, as these slopes can catch you off guard and are suspect of sliding in surprising ways.
discussion:
Following a week of well below-average temperatures, yesterday's sunshine resulted in southerly slopes feeling like spring. However, air temperatures remained cool with just one mid or upper-elevation station, Hornet (6500'), warming above freezing.  
As expected, the strong mid-February sun affected the cold, dry snow surface on steep southerly aspects. Most observations noted loose snow slides that ran on Friday or Saturday (Observation), with one party witnessing a D2 dry loose slide in action (Observation). At the same time, another group watched a small dry loose trigger a storm slab, resulting in a D2 slide (Observation). 
On Saturday, two parties in the Marion Lake area above Essex unintentionally triggered large slab avalanches. Our limited knowledge of one incident where a member was injured is found here. A skier triggered a slide that caught another member of the party below. The victim was carried through small trees and came to rest, partially buried and injured. Two Bear Air extracted the individual, who was treated for serious but non-life-threatening injuries. Another party had skied a nearby slope earlier in the day. An Extended Column Test they conducted before skiing scored ECTN; it broke but did not propagate on an interface between storm layers within 2 feet of the surface. We believe the slide was on an east aspect above the head of the lake, but we are not sure of the elevation. We will update our website with more information as it becomes available.
A thorough observation by the other party is here. This observation contains a wealth of information, and we appreciate this group for sharing this information in the name of public safety. This avalanche fractured with the third skier on the slope at the skier's skis and ran full track through a convoluted avalanche path. The slide was on a north aspect at 7100' and failed as a hard slab on a layer of facets above the early February crust. The party noted, "The avalanche caught us completely off guard, given our other observations throughout the day on similar aspects and at similar elevations." They also shared this pearl of wisdom, "Please keep heads up out there and don't get lulled into a false sense of security because you aren't finding the problem everywhere - that's the sneaky part about persistent weak layers."
The bluebird weather allowed for views of avalanche activity associated with the late-January and early February warming and atmospheric river along with the February 11 and 12 storm cycle. Check out these observations for more details (Observation 1, Observation 2, Observation 3).

bottom_line: Natural loose snow avalanches may occur on steep slopes that face the sun or are initiated by riders on shady aspects. It remains possible to trigger a 1-2 foot thick slab avalanche on a buried weak layer where the surface snow is consolidated. Pay attention to changing snow surface conditions throughout the day.
discussion:
A Bluebird Saturday is on tap to reward the hard-working mid-week employees. Another cool day is expected, although warmer than yesterday. This morning, temperatures at mid and upper elevations are generally several degrees warmer except in the eastern part of the advisory area, where Marias Pass is +29 F and Shed 7 +16 F from yesterday.
A day of sun and warmer temperatures may initiate natural loose snow avalanches on sunny slopes, especially this afternoon. In most locations, the tree branches still hold substantial snow, as I observed above Essex yesterday. Today's warming may shed this snow, initiating rollerballs or loose slides upon impact with the surface snow.  Rock outcroppings facing the sun will heat and possibly shed their snow cover. These slides will gain mass as they move downhill, perhaps running fast and far in the unconsolidated surface snow. Loose slides in the 1-2 feet of soft surface snow can be triggered by riders on shaded slopes, as this group experienced in Cascadilla yesterday. 
Today's warming may consolidate the surface snow, forming a denser slab on top of potentially weak layers. Note where your boards or machines do not sink as deeply into the snow surface and steer around these areas in favor of softer snow or lower-angle terrain for a more enjoyable and safer experience. 
Winds have shifted to the southwest after a few days of north and easterly flow. We removed wind slab from the problem list, but in isolated locations, riders may still be able to trigger a slide in the dense wind-drifted snow on atypical aspects. An experienced rider identified and avoided these in the southern Whitefish Range in favor of soft boot top powder. 
Upcoming event: Flathead Avalanche Friends Fest, February 21-24. We have four days of events on tap for you: Beacons & Brews, Name That Tune/Name That Avalanche, Workshop Night with State of the Snowpack and USGS, Hot Dog Days, and the Ultimate Après with Backcountry Partner Speed Dating and the Big A$$ Raffle. Cracking around your boards or machines indicates that wind-blown snow is sensitive to human triggering. bottom_line: Safely enjoy today’s sunshine by recognizing—and steering away from— wind-drifted snow along alpine ridges. Use small and inconsequential slopes to test recent snow before committing to steep terrain. Reduce the consequences of a slide by limiting travel above hazards like gullies and cliffs.
discussion:
Dry conditions today should keep us on a trend toward stability.  Snowfall from early this week continues to settle on top of the remarkably stout and well-documented crust from early February.  However, weak faceted snow and patchy surface hoar on or near this crust remain a concern, particularly on shaded slopes above 6500' (where we've had limited recent observations). The last reported activity on these layers was Tuesday (Flathead Range, here). Wednesday, several parties felt comfortable transitioning to a "stepping out" mindset by riding slopes greater than 30 degrees in steepness though they still avoided likely trigger points and terrain traps. With great riding conditions and forecasted blue skies, I expect folks will be getting after it today and tomorrow.  If you're one of those folks, consider starting small before committing to larger and steeper slopes.  Also, let us know what you find during your travels: we received no observations from yesterday. Even reports of benign conditions are useful.
Our Canadian neighbors are reporting a similar but more active snowpack.  On Wednesday, A skier near Fernie triggered a large (D2) slab avalanche on a southeast aspect at 6400'.  Also on Wednesday, explosives mitigation near Fernie produced many large (D2) avalanches in the alpine on nearly all aspects.
Upcoming event: Flathead Avalanche Friends Fest, February 21-24. We have four days of events on tap for you: Beacons & Brews, Name That Tune/Name That Avalanche, Workshop Night with State of the Snowpack and USGS, Hot Dog Days, and the Ultimate Après with Backcountry Partner Speed Dating and the Big A$$ Raffle.
